topic: Sharp partial identification of distributional treatment effects via optimal-transport extremal couplings under marginal-overlap restrictions on counterfactual outcome distributions

open_problems.open_problem: Characterize the sharp identified set for the full treatment-effect CDF under covariate-specific marginal-overlap restrictions on the counterfactual outcome marginals.

source_refs.claim: Makarov bounds are sharp at one CDF point but not uniformly sharp for finite vectors or functionals of the treatment-effect CDF, and covariates can tighten bounds.
source_refs.author: Firpo and Ridder

source_refs.claim: The paper gives sharp DTE bounds from identified potential-outcome marginals and notes covariates can tighten the bounds.
source_refs.author: Fan and Park

source_refs.excerpt: marginal DSC identifies F0 iff the estimand is marginal, while F_Delta is point identified iff
otherwise must be replaced by the LP/Frechet-Makarov envelope

open_problems.why_unsolved: The published literature separates pointwise Makarov sharpness, finite-grid improvements, and covariate averaging, while the prior reviewer flagged exactly the missing marginal-versus-joint frontier for DTE reporting. The unresolved obstacle is simultaneous sharpness across CDF points when covariate-specific overlap restrictions bind differently and the feasible copula cannot be optimized independently point by point.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A theorem giving the sharp set of admissible DTE CDF paths as an overlap-constrained coupling problem, with an equivalent finite or continuum OT dual and conditions for when covariate aggregation is uniformly sharp.

open_problems.open_problem: Develop a finite-support LP/OT diagnostic for DTE reporting from marginal counterfactual curves, with explicit variables, constraints, dimension, and bound width for Pr{Delta <= d}.

source_refs.claim: The paper bounds continuous functionals of the joint potential-outcome distribution and emphasizes computational considerations for sharp bounds with additional constraints.
source_refs.author: Thomas M. Russell

source_refs.claim: Sharp identified sets for moments of the joint potential-outcome distribution are intervals whose endpoints are optimal-transport values.
source_refs.author: Daniel Ober-Reynolds

source_refs.excerpt: Add a computable finite-support LP/OT diagnostic with explicit dimension and constraints

open_problems.why_unsolved: General computational partial-identification machinery exists for joint-potential-outcome functionals, but the reviewer found the DTE marginal-curve proposal below flagship without an operational diagnostic. The concrete missing piece is a small, auditable LP/OT object whose dimension and constraints are tied to marginal-overlap cells and whose width directly tells a practitioner when marginal DTE reporting is unsupported.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A sharp finite LP theorem for lower and upper bounds on Pr{Delta <= d}, including a dimension formula, overlap/coupling constraints, and a proof that the diagnostic width is zero exactly in the point-identified reporting cases.

open_problems.open_problem: Classify dependence restrictions on potential outcomes that make a marginally identified counterfactual DTE point identified, testable, or sharply bounded under marginal overlap.

source_refs.claim: The paper studies partial identification of general functionals of the joint distribution of potential outcomes and characterizes when covariates shrink identified sets.
source_refs.author: Fan, Guerre and Zhu

source_refs.claim: They bound DTEs using mutually stochastically increasing potential outcomes and show the dependence assumption has testable implications.
source_refs.author: Frandsen and Lefgren

source_refs.excerpt: F_Delta is point identified iff a named copula condition is imposed

open_problems.why_unsolved: Positive dependence, rank invariance, copula equality, monotone response, and supermodularity tighten DTE bounds through different mathematical channels. The literature contains separate sufficient restrictions, while the prior proposal asks for the strict iff frontier; what is missing is a single taxonomy that states which restrictions are testable from marginal/covariate data, which are only sensitivity restrictions, and exactly how each changes the sharp DTE set.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A lattice of assumption classes with nesting, falsifiability tests, necessary-and-sufficient point-identification conditions, and sharp DTE identified-set formulas for each class.

open_problems.open_problem: Prove or refute sharp pasting for lifted OT relaxations that combine marginal sensitivity envelopes, IV or dose restrictions, and local rank-copula tubes into one full-data law for distributional treatment effects.

source_refs.claim: PRTE partial identification under limited IV support is formulated as constrained conditional OT and reduced to sharp one-dimensional OT subproblems.
source_refs.author: Tan, Blanchet and Syrgkanis

source_refs.claim: The paper studies partial identification of the treatment-effect distribution in switching-regime models and confidence sets.
source_refs.author: Fan and Wu

source_refs.excerpt: finite lifted optimal-transport relaxation that is a valid outer envelope
pasting theorem showing when the envelope is sharp for one full-data law

open_problems.why_unsolved: Recent CCOT results show sharpness for some policy-relevant mean functionals, and older switching-regime work handles DTE bounds in more structured settings. The prior rejected angle isolates the harder pasting issue: separately feasible marginal/dosewise OT tables may fail to be jointly realizable by a single full-data law once rank-copula tubes and sensitivity envelopes are imposed simultaneously.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A sharpness theorem constructing a single admissible full-data law for every feasible lifted OT table, or a counterexample with a precise regime statement showing the relaxation is only an outer bound.

open_problems.open_problem: Obtain uniformly valid inference for overlap-constrained DTE bound processes when counterfactual marginals, propensity scores, and overlap sets are estimated.

source_refs.claim: The paper studies inference for sharp DTE bounds and reports nonregular bootstrap behavior requiring alternatives such as m-out-of-n bootstrap.
source_refs.author: Fan and Park

source_refs.claim: Inference for partially identified interval parameters is delicate because endpoint and confidence-region coverage do not coincide under nuisance uncertainty.
source_refs.author: Joerg Stoye

source_refs.claim: Calibrated projection confidence intervals provide uniform coverage for functions of partially identified parameter vectors.
source_refs.author: Kaido, Molinari and Stoye

open_problems.why_unsolved: Overlap-constrained DTE bounds combine estimated conditional distributions with nonsmooth infimum/supremum operators and support constraints that may be nearly binding. Existing interval and moment-inequality inference theory does not directly provide practical uniform bands for the entire lower and upper bound functions in this degenerating-overlap regime.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A weak-limit and bootstrap or subsampling theorem for the full lower and upper DTE-bound processes, valid uniformly over regimes with fixed, near-binding, and estimated marginal-overlap constraints.

open_problems.open_problem: Extend sharp overlap-constrained DTE bounds to discrete, count-valued, and mixed outcome supports without relying on continuous-outcome approximations.

source_refs.claim: Count-valued outcomes create subtle considerations beyond the continuous case, and the paper derives appropriate bounds for DTEs with count outcomes.
source_refs.author: John Mullahy

source_refs.claim: The paper gives sharp bounds for relative treatment effects with ordinal outcomes from the fixed marginal distributions of potential outcomes.
source_refs.author: Lu, Ding and Dasgupta

source_refs.claim: Pointwise Makarov bounds need not sharply bound functionals of the treatment-effect CDF, so discrete-grid functionals require their own sharp analysis.
source_refs.author: Firpo and Ridder

open_problems.why_unsolved: Mass points and lattice supports make rearrangement and inversion arguments non-generic; overlap restrictions can also eliminate or merge feasible cells in the transportation table. Existing count and ordinal results cover important special cases, but not a general marginal-overlap DTE formula spanning mixed supports.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A discrete OT or network-flow characterization of all feasible treatment-effect distributions with fixed discrete or mixed marginals and overlap restrictions, with computable sharp bounds and an inference recipe.

literature_map:
Makarov (1981) and Rueschendorf (1982) are the probability anchors: fixed marginals imply sharp pointwise bounds for sums or differences through extremal couplings.
Heckman, Smith and Clements (1997) brought the issue to program evaluation, stressing that mean impacts miss individual-effect heterogeneity.
Fan and Park (2010) give sharp DTE bounds and inference when potential-outcome marginals are identified, including covariate refinements.
Firpo and Ridder (2019) sharpen the central tension: Makarov bounds are pointwise but not uniformly sharp for CDF paths or functionals, and covariates can improve bounds.
Fan, Guerre and Zhu (2017) broaden the problem to functionals of the joint potential-outcome distribution and characterize when covariates shrink identified sets.
Russell (2021) and Ober-Reynolds (2023) provide computational and OT routes for bounding joint-potential-outcome functionals, but not the specific marginal-overlap DTE diagnostic.
Frandsen and Lefgren (2021), Kim (2017), and Manski-style monotonicity work show that dependence and shape restrictions can tighten bounds, but their testable/sharp taxonomy remains incomplete.
Fan and Yu (2012), Frandsen (2020), and recent panel-DTE work show panel information can restrict dependence, raising a separate panel-coupling frontier.
Mullahy (2023) and ordinal-outcome work show that count and discrete supports are not trivial continuous special cases.
Stoye (2009), Chernozhukov-Hong-Tamer (2007), and Kaido-Molinari-Stoye (2019) clarify partial-identification inference, but overlap-constrained DTE-bound processes remain nonregular.
Recent OT work by Fan-Park-Xu, Fan-Pass-Shi, and Tan-Blanchet-Syrgkanis suggests constrained OT is the right language for the next DTE sharpness step.
The live gap for this qid is the overlap-restricted coupling geometry: how known or bounded counterfactual marginals, support/overlap constraints, and copula restrictions jointly determine sharp DTE sets.
prior_proposal_map:
Inspected pid_qte_marginal_sensitivity/v1 proposal, rejected angles, README, and reviews; yielded adjacent CQTE tail-quotient and finite-certificate problems but these were less directly DTE-coupling specific.
Inspected eid_dist_synth_control/v1 bank state, README, proposal-review JSONs; yielded the closest DTE marginal-versus-copula and LP/Frechet-Makarov diagnostic problems.
Inspected pid_dose_response_iv/v1 proposal, rejected angle0, README, and reviews; yielded lifted OT/coupling pasting and copula-radius sign-boundary problems.
Scanned broader bank/review text for coupling, Makarov, DTE, distributional, and marginal keywords; pid_mte_multi_iv was adjacent LP geometry but less directly tied to DTE couplings.
Active pid_ot_dte_marginal research directory only had state/pipeline init files, so it did not itself yield prior-proposal candidates.
Study-pipeline gaps were read as augmenting context; most were LATE or Manski interval-regression gaps, with the inference-for-bound-intersections theme retained through the web-grounded DTE inference entry.
